coredata.dc:title: Dual amplification electrochemiluminescence of carbonized polymer dots embedded in the metal-organic framework with low trigger potential and nanoconfinement enhancement for sensitive CYFRA21-1 immunoassay
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jingjing
Fang, Jinglong
Li, Min
Liu, Lei
Cao, Wei
Wei, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Developing novel luminophores for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) that could operate smoothly under low-potential excitation while incorporating creative enhancement strategies is a challenging but very attractive initiative to achieve sensitive detection of disease markers. Herein, carbonized polymer dots (L-CPDs) with low cathode excitation potential were successfully synthesized and used for dual amplified sensitive electrochemiluminescence immunoassay of cytokeratin 19 fragment antigen 21–1 (CYFRA21–1). l-CPDs were first used as ECL luminophores and have an unprecedentedly low excitation potential of -0.95 V that has not been equipped for other cathodic carbon-base dots, avoiding side reactions, bioactive damage and electrode polarization due to high potential, thus obtaining excellent sensor performance. In addition, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) synthesized at room temperature as a nanoconfinement carrier encapsulated l-CPDs and reduced the non-radiative energy loss of the luminophore, which led to substantially improved the ECL signal. The ECL efficiency of the encapsulated luminophores is 2.7 times higher than that of monomeric l-CPDs at the same mass concentration. Platinum composite iron oxide (Fe3O4/Pt) was used as a co-reaction acceleration platform to strengthen the ECL response. The sandwich ECL immunosensor fabricated achieved sensitive detection of CYFRA21–1 in the detection range of 150 fg/mL–110 ng/mL with a detection limit of 126 fg/mL. In conclusion, this work provides a consideration direction of developing ideal luminophores complemented by amplification strategies in ECL systems construction to achieve accurate and sensitive detection of targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Carbonized polymer dots
Low cathode excitation potential
CYFRA21-1
Nanoconfinement

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of L-CPDs, L-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au, and Fe3O4
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of Fe3O4/Pt
Spectral characterization of L-CPDs and L-CPDs@ZIF-8
Stability, selectivity, and reproducibility of biosensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province [Grant No. ZR2022MB089 ]; the Natural Science Foundation for Young of Shandong Province [ZR2020QB093]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 22274062 ]; the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Projects of China [Grant No. 21627809 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of l-CPDs, (B) SEM image of l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au, (C) FT-IR spectra of l-CPDs, l-CPDs@ZIF-8, l-Trp, (D) HRTEM image of Au NPs, (E) SEM image of Fe3O4, (F) XRD pattern Fe3O4.
XPS spectra of the Fe3O4/Pt catalysts. (A) Survey XPS spectra, (B) Fe, (C) O, and (D) Pt spectra.
(A) PL spectra of l-CPDs in water and (B) ECL spectra of l-CPDs and l-CPDs@ZIF-8 in K2S2O8.
(A) EIS spectra of the substrate and (B) lable. (C) ECL intensity-potential curves and (D) ECL intensity−time curves.

(A) CV curves of the stepwise characterization of the constructed biosensor and (B) EIS of the stepwise characterization of the proposed biosensor: (a: bare GCE, b: GCE + Fe3O4/Pt, c: GCE + Fe3O4/Pt + Ab1, d: GCE + Fe3O4/Pt+Ab1 + BSA, e: GCE + Fe3O4/Pt + Ab1 + BSA + CYFRA21–1, f: GCE + Fe3O4/Pt + Ab1 + BSA + CYFRA21-1 + L-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au-Ab2). (C and D) ECL curves and equivalent response. Immunosensors for the detection of different concentrations of CYFRA21–1: 150 fg/mL, 2 pg/mL, 8 pg/mL, 20 pg/mL, 200 pg/mL, 800 pg/mL, 6 ng/mL, 20 ng/mL, and 110 ng/mL. (E) Signal stability of the constructed immunosensor. (F) Selectivity.
The schematic illustration of the electrochemiluminescence immunosensor.
Detection of CYFRA21–1 in serum samples.

head.ce:title: Dual amplification electrochemiluminescence of carbonized polymer dots embedded in the metal-organic framework with low trigger potential and nanoconfinement enhancement for sensitive CYFRA21-1 immunoassay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 16419, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Sungkyunkwan University, Suwon 16419, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing novel luminophores for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) that could operate smoothly under low-potential excitation while incorporating creative enhancement strategies is a challenging but very attractive initiative to achieve sensitive detection of disease markers. Herein, carbonized polymer dots (L-CPDs) with low cathode excitation potential were successfully synthesized and used for dual amplified sensitive electrochemiluminescence immunoassay of cytokeratin 19 fragment antigen 21–1 (CYFRA21–1). l-CPDs were first used as ECL luminophores and have an unprecedentedly low excitation potential of -0.95 V that has not been equipped for other cathodic carbon-base dots, avoiding side reactions, bioactive damage and electrode polarization due to high potential, thus obtaining excellent sensor performance. In addition, zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) synthesized at room temperature as a nanoconfinement carrier encapsulated l-CPDs and reduced the non-radiative energy loss of the luminophore, which led to substantially improved the ECL signal. The ECL efficiency of the encapsulated luminophores is 2.7 times higher than that of monomeric l-CPDs at the same mass concentration. Platinum composite iron oxide (Fe3O4/Pt) was used as a co-reaction acceleration platform to strengthen the ECL response. The sandwich ECL immunosensor fabricated achieved sensitive detection of CYFRA21–1 in the detection range of 150 fg/mL–110 ng/mL with a detection limit of 126 fg/mL. In conclusion, this work provides a consideration direction of developing ideal luminophores complemented by amplification strategies in ECL systems construction to achieve accurate and sensitive detection of targets.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lung cancer is the most common primary malignancy of the lung with very high morbidity and mortality, of which non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the major subtype, accounting for approximately 85% of all lung cancers [1–3]. NSCLC develops rapidly in advanced stages and is difficult to treat, so searching for sensitive biomarkers that can diagnose NSCLC early is significant to reduce lung cancer mortality. Among the currently known biomarkers, cytokeratin 19 fragment antigen 21–1 (CYFRA21–1) is believed to have driven the progress of early diagnosis and even treatment of NSCLC [4,5]. To date, various methods have been established for detecting CYFRA21–1, for instance, surface plasma resonance [6], mass spectrometry (MS) [7], flow cytometry (FCM) [8], electrochemical immunosensors [9], etc. However, these methods have some limitations, such as high analytical costs, expensive equipment, complicated operation, and poor sensitivity. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a process of free radical interactions triggered by electrochemical stimulation and plays an important role in immunoassay [10], genetic analysis [11], protein analysis [12], and other fields [13–15]. With the advantages of low background noise, simple operation, high sensitivity and good controllability, it is an excellent technique for achieving CYFRA21–1 detection [16–19].

To further improve the ECL sensing performance, many signal amplification strategies have been developed, such as nano-enzyme catalysis [20], DNA-assisted amplification [21], efficient ECL luminophores [22], surface enhancement [23], etc. ECL luminophores with good luminescence properties play a crucial role in the construction of highly sensitive immunosensors. In recent years, carbon quantum dots ((graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)) have attracted wide interest from researchers due to their relatively stable chemistry properties, controllable wavelength range, efficient quantum yields and good biocompatibility [24–27]. For example, Jampasa et al. based on nitrogen-decorated carbon dots constructed ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence sensor for Listeria monocytogenes determination [28]. Liu et al. used fluorine-nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as an ECL emitter for ultrasensitive detection of HIV-DNA fragment [29]. Notably, carbon-based dots currently used for cathodic electrochemiluminescence are often accompanied by high excitation potentials, which leads to inevitable electrode passivation and interference caused by redox substances in biological substances, and even irreversible damage to antigen-antibody activity. Therefore, the exploration of low-potential-triggered luminophores complemented by proven amplification strategies is significant for sensitive, efficient and applicable immunosensors.

In addition, the use of nanomaterials as catalysts for luminescence amplification enhancement is a very effective tactic. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a common MOF with plentiful metal ions and carbon and nitrogen ligands, rich pore channels, as well as large specific surface area, which facilitates electron transport and surface loading in electrocatalytic reaction [30–34]. The enclosed space at the nanoscale provides a suitable microenvironment for chemical reactions. Specifically, a confined and stable porous structure increases the accumulation and collision of intermediate radicals, shortens the chemical reaction distance, and reduces non-radiative energy dissipation [35–37]. Therefore, it is an ideal reactive carrier for the construction of ECL immunosensors. Based on this consideration, platinum composite iron oxide (Fe3O4/Pt) was introduced into the bioimmunosensing system and synergized with ZIF-8 for dual amplification of the ECL signal. Fe3O4 has a prominent catalytic effect on redox reactions, and its rough surface increases the reactive sites considerably [38–40]. K2S2O8 was catalyzed by the abundant Fe2+/Fe3+ redox pair, which produced a large amount of SO4 ·−. And the Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) on the surface as excellent electron conduction mediators, further improved the catalytic performance of Fe3O4 [41].

In this paper, low potential-triggered cathodoluminescent l-CPDs equipped with a nanoconfinement enhancement strategy were designed for the construction of an immunosensor for the detection of CYFRA21–1 in human serum. l-CPDs exhibited a relatively desirable ECL signal at a lower excitation potential avoiding the background interference from the involvement of biological substances and airborne oxygen in the reaction. Its ECL signal is doubly amplified by the nanoconfinement effect of ZIF-8 and the excellent electronic conductivity of Au NPs, which has a wide detection range and high target detection accuracy. The nanoconfinement enhancement of ZIF-8 resulted in a 2.7-fold amplification of the ECL signal of the marker. In pursuit of more sensitive detection, Fe3O4 was introduced into the constructed bioimmunosensing system. The presence of Fe2+/Fe3+ redox pairs in Fe3O4 increased the electron transfer rate and accelerated the production of SO4 ·−. As expected, the constructed biosensing platform has achieved sensitive detection of CYFRA21–1 in the range of 150 fg/ mL – 110 ng/mL with a detection limit as low as 126 fg/mL and good selectivity and stability. Therefore, cathodic low potential triggered l-CPDs has a promising application as a luminophore in the fields of immunosensors.

The synthesis of l-CPDs refers to the methods reported in the literature [42]. Typically, 2.58 mmol l-tryptophan (L-Trp) and 3.47 mmol o-phenylenediamine (oPD) were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 30 min. 1.5 mL concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was slowly added to the above mixture, stirred for 2 min, and transferred to a 50 mL teflon-lined autoclave was heated at 160 °C for 7 h After the reaction kettle was naturally cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a filter membrane (0.22 μm). The product was dialyzed with a 250 Da dialysis bag, and finally freeze-dried for 48 h The obtained solids were stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for use.

The synthesis of l-CPDs@ZIF-8 refers to the reported synthesis methods with a few modifications [43]. 5.0 mL (102.5 mg) of dimethylimidazole and 5.0 mL (1.0 mg) of l-CPDs were mixed into a homogeneous solution and 0.5 mL of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (1.0 M) was added rapidly to the above solution. After being stirred for 40 min and left to stand for 24 h at room temperature, the obtained solid products were collected by centrifugation and washed more than three times repeatedly with deionized water. Finally, L-CPDs@ZIF-8 nanoparticles were obtained by freeze-drying at -40 °C. The synthesis procedure for l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au was the same as that for L-CPDs@ZIF-8, except that 40 uL of Au NPs was added after 5 min of stirring.

1.0 mL of PBS (pH=7.4) containing 10 mg of ZIF-8@L-CPDs/Au was mixed with 500 μL of detection antibody (Ab2) solution (1.0 μg/mL) and shaken continuously at 4 °C for 24 h After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm, l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au-Ab2 was obtained and stored at 4 °C for constructing the biosensor.

The construction process of the immunosensor was shown in Scheme 1 . First, the surface of the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished with different sizes of Al2O3 polishing powders, and washed with deionized water. A substrate solution was obtained by adding 3.0 mg of Fe3O4/Pt to the PBS buffer solution (1.0 mL, pH = 7.4). Then 6 μL of the well-mixed substrate solution was pipetted onto the clean surface of GCE and dried at room temperature. Subsequently, 6 μL of Ab1 solution (1 μg/mL) was added dropwise onto the dried substrate surface and incubated for 1 h at 4 °C. The subsequent assembly steps were rinsed with PBS (0.1 M, pH=7.4) to remove unbound biomass. In the next step, 1% BSA (2 μL) was dropped on the surface of the above material to block the exposed non-specific active site and then dried at 4 °C. Finally, after incubation with different concentrations of CYFRA21–1 antigen solution (6 μL) at room temperature, the electrodes were modified with l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au-Ab2 bio-labeler coupling (6 μL) to form sandwich-type immunosensors. The constructed immunosensors were stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for use. The optimization of the experimental conditions is described in detail in Fig. S1 of the supporting material.

The ECL signals of the constructed immunosensors were measured in PBS (10.0 mL, pH=7.4) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 with the MPI-E electrochemiluminescence analyzer. The cyclic voltammetry method was applied to obtain the ECL signal by scanning the electrodes at a scan rate of 0.15 V/s in the range of -0.95–0 V. The ECL test was performed under optimal conditions where the photomultiplier voltage was set to 750 V and the number of amplification stages was set to 4. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the lower the test potential, the less the signal interference from the side reactions under the same measurement conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of L-CPDs, L-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au, and Fe3O4
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the morphological characteristics of the luminophores, L-CPDs and L-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM), respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 A, L-CPDs is quasi-spherical with distinct carbon cores and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The infrared image in Fig. 1C shows that the sample surface is rich in functional groups. It is proved to be a typical carbonized polymer structure. Typically, the absorption peak at 3475 cm−1 corresponds to OH and/or NH stretching vibration, which becomes broader compared to L-Trp, and the absorption peak at 1735 cm−1 is C=O stretching vibration. Both absorption peaks have a slight red shift, indicating the possible existence of carboxylic acid and amide structures in the compound [42]. The absorption peaks of l-CPDs are stronger in C=C and weaker in C=O than those of l-Trp, which may be due to dehydration during the reaction process, and the additional -CONH peaks are the result of the reaction between amino and carboxyl groups. It can be observed from the SEM images in Fig. 1B that l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au have a uniform size distribution with a length of about 600 nm (Fig. S3A) and a width of about 135 nm (Fig. S3B). Both Figs. 1B and S4A demonstrate that the surface of l-CPDs@ZIF-8 was loaded with abundant Au NPs. Fig. 1D confirms that the synthesized Au NPs have a uniform ellipsoidal structure. In contrast to l-CPDs, the absence of functional groups in l-CPDs@ZIF-8 proved that l-CPDs were encapsulated inside rather than being bound to the surface of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1C). The microscopic morphology of Fe3O4 was characterized by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1E, Fe3O4 is a homogeneous spherical structure with a rough surface and the average particle size is about 240 nm. The rough surface structure and small particle size further increase the specific surface area of the catalyst. The lattice structure of Fe3O4 was characterized by x-ray diffraction in Fig. 1F. Strong diffraction peaks were observed at 2θ = 18.3°, 30.1°, 35.4°, 43.1°, 53.4°, 56.9°, and 62.5°, which can be attributed to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planar diffraction magnetite structure of Fe3O4 (JCPDS No. 99–0073). Figs. S4B, S5, and S6 indicated the successful preparation of Fe3O4/Pt composites synthesized by the template-free method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of Fe3O4/Pt
ce:section.ce:para: XPS was used to study the chemical state and valence state of surface elements on Fe3O4/Pt. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the XPS data of Fe3O4/Pt has two peaks at 710.88 eV and 724.48 eV, which are linked to the Fe 2P3/2 and Fe 2P1/2 binding energies of Fe3O4/Pt, respectively [44]. The higher binding energy corresponds to multiple states of Fe3+ and Fe2+ components on the Fe3O4/Pt catalyst surface, where Fe2+ is effective in activating potassium persulfate (PDS) [45]. The O 1S XPS spectrum shows a primary peak of 530.28 eV and a secondary peak of 531.58 eV (Fig. 2C). The main peak can be attributed to the lattice oxygen of Fe3O4/Pt and the secondary peak can be attributed to the surface adsorption of oxygen. In addition, Pt 4f5/2 and Pt 4f7/2 of Fe3O4/Pt are at 71.72 and 75.00 eV, correspondingly (Fig. 2D). The presence of Pt NPs greatly enhanced the electron transfer rate on the surface of the nanosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of L-CPDs and L-CPDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the l-CPDs are measured by photoluminescence (PL) and ECL spectroscopy, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the PL excitation peak of l-CPDs is 572 nm and the emission peak is 620 nm. Under the excitation light at 365 nm, the l-CPDs shows intense pink fluorescence (Fig. S7). In Fig. 3B, an ECL scan is performed at a constant potential of −0.95 V to explore the wavelength position of the emission light of l-CPDs and l-CPDs@ZIF-8. The obtained spectrum shows the maximum emission peak at 644 nm. Compared with the PL emission spectra, the ECL spectra of l-CPDs are redshifted by 24 nm. We believe that this may be due to the fact that the surface defect forbidden band width of l-CPDs is smaller than that inside the nucleus, so the ECL spectrum emitted from the surface appears redshifted compared to the PL spectrum emitted from the inner nucleus [46–48].

When the surface of the electrode is modified with Fe3O4 nanospheres, the effective active area of the electrode surface is greatly increased due to the rough structure of the ball surface, which provides abundant active sites for the loading of Pt NPs. Notably, as shown in Fig. 4 A, the electrode modified by Fe3O4/Pt shows a smaller semicircle than the electrode modified by Fe3O4, indicating that the Fe3O4/Pt surface has a better charge transfer capability. Fig. S8 also confirms that the modification of Pt NPs on the Fe3O4 surface enhances the electrical conductivity of the material. The electron transfer from platinum atoms to the Fe3O4 superficial is believed to be the cause of its enhanced catalytic activity [49]. Similarly, the modification of Au accelerates the injection of hot electrons thus reducing the resistance of l-CPDs@ZIF-8 [50] (Fig. 4B). The ECL behaviors of l-CPDs/K2S2O8, l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8, Fe3O4-l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8, and Fe3O4/Pt-l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8 are investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 4C and D. From these data, it can be seen that the ECL signal of l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8 is twice as strong as that of l-CPDs/K2S2O8. It may be that the confinement effect of nanopores in ZIF-8 increases the interaction and collision probability between radicals, which remarkably enhances the ECL emission. In addition, the ECL signal of Fe3O4-l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8 is 1.4 times higher than that of l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8, demonstrating the catalytic effect of Fe3O4. The ECL signal of Fe3O4/Pt-l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8 is 1.8 times higher than that of l-CPDs@ZIF-8/K2S2O8, which indicates that the catalytic effect of Fe3O4/Pt in the K2S2O8 system is more effective than that of Fe3O4. Due to the synergistic effect between ZIF-8 and Fe3O4/Pt, the catalytic reduction response signal of K2S2O8 is further amplified, and the sandwich-type immunosensor constructed based on the K2S2O8 system obtain good detection effect.

The possible ECL mechanism of the immunosensor constructed by l-CPDs is shown in Fig. S9. First, as shown in Fig. S9A, under the condition without the presence of co-reactants, l-CPDs could not generate effective ECL signal. After K2S2O8 was introduced as a co-reactant, a small portion of S2O8 2− was reduced to SO4 ·− on the electrode surface, which reacted with the l-CPDs·− reduced from l-CPDs to form l-CPDs⁎. However, l-CPDs⁎ was unstable and quickly migrated back to the ground state, generating an ECL signal during this process (Fig. S9B). As is well known the process of electron reduction of K2S2O8 to SO4 ·− at the electrode surface is slow (Eqs 1), which means that a certain catalyst is required to facilitate the conversion process. In this case, Fe3O4 was introduced as a catalyst, in which Fe2+ can effectively catalyze the reduction of S2O8 2− to abundant SO4 ·− (Eqs 2). Meanwhile, the oxidized product Fe3+ can be converted to Fe2+ by getting electrons again, forming a Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle to participate in the catalytic reaction (Eqs 3). As shown in Fig. S9C, this catalytic process resulted in a significant enhancement of the ECL signal. In conclusion, a possible reaction the mechanism can be described as follows Eqs. (1)–((6)) [28]. After calculation, the ECL efficiency of l-CPDs was calculated to be 16.3% and that of l-CPDs@ZIF-8 was 45.1%.
(1) S2O8 2− + e→SO4 2− + SO4 ·−
(2) Fe2+ + S2O8 2−→Fe3+ + SO4 2− + SO4 ·− (more)
(3) Fe3+ + e→Fe2+
(4) L-CPDs + e→L-CPDs·−
(5) L-CPDs·− + SO4 ·−→L-CPDs* + SO4 2−
(6) L-CPDs*→L-CPDs + hv

To verify the integrity of the immunosensor, the cyclic voltammetric curves (CV) of the stepwise construction of the immunosensor were measured at a scan rate of 0.15 V/s using potassium ferricyanide ( [Fe(CN)6]3−) as the electrolyte. As observed in Fig. 5 A, a pair of distinct redox peaks appeared for bare GCE, and the peak current gradually declined after four successive modifications of Fe3O4/Pt, Ab1, BSA, and CYFRA21–1 on the GCE surface. Nevertheless, the peak current is slightly enhanced after l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au-Ab2 modification, which can be attributed to the excellent electronic conduction properties of ZIF-8 and Au NPs. In Fig. 5B, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the constructed biosensor were measured at an AC amplitude of 0.007 V using potassium ferricyanide ( [Fe(CN)6]3−) and Potassium ferrocyanide ( [Fe(CN)6]4−) as the electrolyte. The resistance (curves b, c, d, and e) increased sequentially with the layerwise modification of the electrode surface materials. The resistance decreased after the final modification of l-CPDs@ZIF-8/Au-Ab2 (curve f). This is consistent with Fig. 5A, demonstrating the successful construction of the immunosensor

The constructed biosensor was used for the ECL response of different concentrations of CYFRA21–1 and the signals were collected in 10 mL of PBS containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 at pH 7.4. As shown in Fig. 5C and D, a good positive correlation was detected between the ECL signal and the logarithm of CYFRA21–1 concentration within the range of 150 fg/mL–110 ng/mL and the detection limit obtained was 126 fg/mL. As can be observed in Fig. 5C, the linear regression equation was I ECL = 11,519.89 + 2886.39 lgc with a regression coefficient of 0.997.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, selectivity, and reproducibility of biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: To study the stability of the configured immunosensor, the electrode was modified with a concentration of 0.2 ng/mL and scanned for 13 cycles. The RSD value of 2.2% was calculated (Fig. 5E), which proved its good stability. Interference experiments were conducted to investigate the selectivity of the biosensor for CYFRA21–1 by taking carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), human serum albumin (HSA), prostate-specific antigen (PSA), procalcitonin (PCT), amyloid-β (Aβ) and AFB1. As shown in Fig. 5F, the ECL signal of the immunosensor composed of a 2-fold concentration of interfering substance instead of the target is much lower than the ECL signal obtained from the target substance. When the target CYFRA21–1 was co-incubated with the interferent, an enhanced ECL signal was achieved, indicating that the constructed biosensor has good selectivity for the detection of CYFRA21–1. After that, seven identical biosensors were constructed and used to detect the ECL signal under the same conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated as 4.8% (Fig. S10), demonstrating good reproducibility of the constructed biosensor. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S11, the immunosensor still equipped with great signals after being stored at 4 °C for 3 weeks.

The efficacy of the immunosensor was evaluated by measuring the concentration of CYFRA21–1 in serum samples. First, a small amount of human serum was taken and the CYFRA21–1 level in the serum was measured using the established immunosensor. The results showed that the CYFRA21–1 level in serum was 1.26 ng/mL. Then, recovery experiments were performed using the standard addition method. Different concentrations of the standard CYFRA21–1 antigen were added to human serum samples during sample preparation. Table 1 shows that the recoveries were in the range of 95.3–102.0% and RSD was in the range of 3.2–4.2% respectively, indicating that the method has excellent precision density and accuracy. Combining the above experimental results, the assembled ECL immunosensor has a broad promising application in clinical diagnosis.

In summary, l-CPDs were synthetically employed as cathodic low potential triggered ECL luminophores and given a dual signal amplification strategy for sandwich type immunosensor analysis. l-CPDs were verified to exhibit significant ECL analysis activity at a trigger potential of -0.95 V and were equipped with a nanoconfinement enhancement strategy using ZIF-8 as an encapsulation carrier to provide multi-fold signal amplification. In addition, Fe3O4/Pt was introduced into the analytical system as a sensing platform, where the redox pair of Fe2+/Fe3+ catalyzed the reduction of K2S2O8 to SO4 ·−, which accelerated the free radical process and thus enabled a second amplification. Ultimately, the developed immunosensor exhibited favorable linearity in the range of 150 fg/mL–110 ng/mL. The detection limit was 126 fg/mL with promising selectivity, stability and reproducibility. Overall, this work provides an effective and robust new approach for the development of practical biosensors for early disease diagnosis, and may also be a potential strategy for the detection of other clinical biomarkers such as aptamers and nucleic acids.

Jingjing Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jinglong Fang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Min Li: Formal analysis, Validation. Lei Liu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wei Cao: Supervision, Formal analysis, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Qin Wei: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province [Grant No. ZR2022MB089]; the Natural Science Foundation for Young of Shandong Province [ZR2020QB093]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [Grant No. 22274062]; the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Projects of China [Grant No. 21627809].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142805.
